wines.name: F.X. Pichler Grüner Veltliner Smaragd Kellerberg
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel and large old oak casks (Fuder); no new oak

pairings.why: The wine's crisp acidity and white-pepper spice cut through the breaded crust and butter.
Mineral salinity echoes the freshwater fish character; the body matches the richness.
Classic Wachau pairing: the wine's herbal notes bridge asparagus bitterness; acidity cuts the sauce.

history.summary: The Kellerberg Ried sits directly above the F.X. Pichler cellars in Oberloiben on deep gneiss and primary rock. F.X. Pichler began bottling it as a single-Ried Smaragd in the early 1980s when the Vinea Wachau quality tier system was being codified.

milestones.event: Vinea Wachau Nobilis Districtus founded; Kellerberg Smaragd becomes part of the inaugural quality tier programme
Lucas Franz Pichler joins the estate, refining fermentation to larger oak Fuder for this cuvée

wines.description: Single-vineyard Grüner Veltliner Smaragd from the Kellerberg Ried above Oberloiben. White pepper, citrus and deep mineral texture from primary-rock gneiss soils.

wines.name: F.X. Pichler Grüner Veltliner Smaragd Loibenberg
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel and large old oak Fuder; no new oak

pairings.why: The wine's full body and mineral core support creamy preparations without being overwhelmed.
Peach and fennel notes harmonise with the delicate herbal character of sea bass.
White pepper and stone fruit in the wine echo the nutty, spiced notes of aged mountain cheese.

history.summary: Loibenberg is one of the largest and best-known Riede in the eastern Wachau, shared among several Loiben producers. F.X. Pichler's parcels on the upper terraces produce a slightly fuller, more generous style than the Kellerberg.

milestones.event: First separate Loibenberg bottling distinguished from the estate's village Grüner Veltliner
Wachau DAC status established; Loibenberg Smaragd qualifies under the new appellation rules

wines.description: Full-bodied Smaragd Grüner Veltliner from the Loibenberg Ried, showing ripe stone fruit and white-pepper spice on a mineral backbone.

wines.name: F.X. Pichler Riesling Smaragd Kellerberg

pairings.why: The wine's racy acidity and mineral depth are a natural partner to delicate crustacean sweetness.
Smokiness in the fish finds a mineral echo in the Riesling; the acidity refreshes between bites.
High acidity and salinity make a compelling match for raw fish.

history.summary: The Kellerberg Riesling from F.X. Pichler is produced from the same Ried as the Grüner Veltliner but expresses the site's gneiss terroir through a racy, mineral profile, with tension and longevity sustained by the primary-rock soils.

milestones.event: First single-Ried Kellerberg Riesling Smaragd bottled separately from the estate's general Riesling
Kellerberg Riesling Smaragd gains international distribution through specialist Wachau importers in the US and UK

wines.description: Racy, stony Smaragd Riesling from the Kellerberg Ried above Oberloiben. Slate mineral, lime zest and dried apricot with exceptional ageing potential.

wines.name: F.X. Pichler Riesling Smaragd Loibner Berg

pairings.why: Citrus notes in both the dish and wine create harmony; the mineral finish refreshes.
Creamy rice softens the wine's acidity; herbal asparagus echoes the wine's green notes.
High acidity cuts through the cream; citrus in the wine lifts the tangy cheese.

history.summary: The Loibner Berg bottling draws from parcels on the broader Loiben slope above the Danube, producing a more immediately accessible Riesling Smaragd with floral and stone-fruit character compared to the more austere Kellerberg site.

milestones.event: First vintage bottled as a separate Loibner Berg Riesling Smaragd cuvée

wines.description: Approachable Smaragd Riesling from the Loibner Berg Ried, with peach blossom, citrus and clean mineral character. Excellent early-drinking but capable of ageing.

wines.name: F.X. Pichler Grüner Veltliner Smaragd M
wines.oak_regime: Large old oak Fuder (500L+); no new oak

pairings.why: The wine's concentration and spice match richly prepared offal; acidity provides counterbalance.
The wine's exotic spice and mineral lift complement delicate shellfish and anise herbs.
Nutty, crystalline aged cheese amplifies the wine's pepper-spice register.

history.summary: The M (formerly labelled Unendlich in some markets) represents the highest tier in the F.X. Pichler range, produced only in outstanding vintages from the best Grüner Veltliner parcels. It embodies the estate's philosophy that Wachau Grüner can achieve the same concentration and longevity as the great Rieslings.

milestones.event: First release of the reserve-tier Grüner Veltliner Smaragd from Kellerberg and Liebenberg parcels
Label redesign consolidates the reserve-tier identity of the M (formerly Unendlich) cuvée

wines.description: Reserve Smaragd Grüner Veltliner from F.X. Pichler's best parcels, produced only in top vintages. Intense pepper spice, concentrated stone fruit and exceptional mineral longevity.

wines.name: F.X. Pichler Grüner Veltliner Smaragd Steinwand
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel and large old Fuder; no new oak

pairings.why: The wine's crisp acidity and pepper-spice energy cut through the fried coating beautifully.
Crunchy acidity and citrus blossom echo the freshness of the greens and vinaigrette.
The wine's mineral salinity and citrus balance the richness of smoked salmon.

history.summary: Steinwand is a single-vineyard site in the Loiben cluster worked by F.X. Pichler on steep primary-rock terraces. The name refers to the stone-wall terrace construction typical of the Wachau's UNESCO-listed vineyards.

milestones.event: First Steinwand bottling from terraced primary-rock parcels above Loiben

wines.description: Crisper, angular Smaragd Grüner Veltliner from the Steinwand Ried: vivid citrus, white pepper and stony mineral. Excellent food wine.

wines.name: F.X. Pichler Riesling Smaragd Steinertal

pairings.why: The wine's racy acidity cuts through rich butter; mineral lift complements the lobster's sweetness.
Blood-orange and lime in the wine resonate with citrus-dressed scallops.
The wine's citrus and gentle petrol note stand up to aromatic Asian preparations.

history.summary: Steinertal is a steep Ried on primary gneiss in the Loiben cluster, shared with Alzinger among others. F.X. Pichler's version captures the most mineral, linear face of the terroir, emphasising tension over richness.

milestones.event: First vintage of the Steinertal Riesling Smaragd as a standalone cuvée

wines.description: Linear, mineral Smaragd Riesling from the Steinertal Ried: petrol, citrus and crushed-slate character with racy acidity and stony length.

wines.name: F.X. Pichler Riesling Smaragd Oberhauser

pairings.why: Floral and herbal aromas in both the dish and wine create an elegant harmony.
Floral aromatics lift the light pasta; the wine's acidity cuts through the butter.
Apricot and citrus in the wine complement the tangy, fresh cheese and vinaigrette.

history.summary: The Oberhauser Ried sits at higher elevation on the Loiben slope, producing Riesling with more delicate floral character compared to the primary-rock intensity of the Kellerberg and Steinertal sites.

milestones.event: First Oberhauser Riesling Smaragd bottled as a standalone single-Ried cuvée

wines.description: Floral, approachable Smaragd Riesling from the Oberhauser Ried above Loiben. Apricot blossom, white flowers and citrus on a clean mineral palate.

wines.name: Emmerich Knoll Riesling Smaragd Loibenberg
wines.oak_regime: Large old oak Fuder; no new oak

pairings.dish: Pan-fried perch with lemon brown butter
pairings.why: Mineral acidity and stone-fruit notes complement freshwater fish perfectly.
Classic Wachau pairing: the wine's mineral spine and fruit handle asparagus bitterness.
Lemon-caper sauce echoes the citrus and herb notes in the wine.

history.summary: The Knoll family has farmed Loibenberg parcels since at least the mid-20th century. Emmerich Knoll established the Loibenberg Riesling Smaragd as a single-site bottling, emphasising traditional Fuder vinification to build complexity.

milestones.event: Loibenberg Riesling Smaragd first bottled as a single-site cuvée
Estate joins the founding Vinea Wachau Nobilis Districtus charter

wines.description: Smaragd Riesling from the Loibenberg Ried: apricot, chalk mineral and bright acidity with a long, fruit-driven finish.

wines.name: Emmerich Knoll Riesling Smaragd Schütt
wines.oak_regime: Large old oak Fuder; no new oak

pairings.why: Petrol and slate notes, combined with racy acidity, are a natural match for briny oysters.
The wine's tight mineral structure cuts through cream while echoing crustacean sweetness.
After 10 years, the Schütt opens into a nutty, textured wine matching aged cheese.

history.summary: The Schütt Ried is a cooler, steeper site in the Loiben cluster that produces the most angular and mineral of the Knoll Rieslings. The name refers to the loose scree (Schutt) that characterises the site's primary rock soil.

milestones.event: First Schütt Riesling Smaragd bottled as a distinct cuvée from the cooler, steeper Schütt parcel

wines.description: Austere, age-worthy Smaragd Riesling from the Schütt Ried: dense slate, petrol and citrus with racy acidity and exceptional ageing potential.

wines.name: Emmerich Knoll Grüner Veltliner Smaragd Loibenberg
wines.oak_regime: Large old oak Fuder; no new oak

pairings.dish: Roast pork with caraway and sauerkraut
pairings.why: White pepper and the wine's weight match the earthy spice of caraway pork.
A Wachau classic pairing: the wine's crisp acidity and pepper spice cut through the breaded veal.
Earthy mushrooms amplify the wine's mineral depth; acidity balances the creamy rice.

history.summary: Emmerich Knoll's Loibenberg Grüner Veltliner is one of the longest-established single-site Grüner Veltliners in the Wachau. The wine demonstrates the Loibenberg's capacity for rich, full-bodied whites from both Riesling and Grüner Veltliner.

milestones.event: First single-vineyard Loibenberg Grüner Veltliner Smaragd from the Knoll estate

wines.description: Full-bodied Smaragd Grüner Veltliner from the Loibenberg: pepper spice, ripe pear and creamy texture with mineral backbone. A Wachau classic.

wines.name: Emmerich Knoll Grüner Veltliner Smaragd Kreutles
wines.oak_regime: Large old oak Fuder; no new oak

pairings.why: Green herb notes in the wine resonate with herb-marinated chicken.
The crisp acidity and herbal quality pair seamlessly with egg and vegetable preparations.
Citrus-forward acidity echoes lime in ceviche; the wine's crisp structure complements raw fish.

history.summary: Kreutles is a higher-elevation site in the Loibenberg area producing a crisper, more herbal expression of Grüner Veltliner compared to the richer Loibenberg Smaragd. It entered the Knoll portfolio as a distinct cuvée in the late 1980s.

milestones.event: Kreutles first bottled as a separate single-Ried Grüner Veltliner Smaragd

wines.description: Delicate, herb-driven Smaragd Grüner Veltliner from the Kreutles site: white pepper, green herbs and yellow apple with crisp, dry finish.

wines.name: Emmerich Knoll Riesling Smaragd Pfaffenberg
wines.oak_regime: Large old oak Fuder; no new oak

pairings.why: Complexity and fine acidity are worthy partners to a luxury crustacean preparation.
After a decade, the wine's richness and mineral complexity match an aged nutty cheese.

pairings.dish: Duck breast with stone fruit sauce
pairings.why: Stone fruit in the wine resonates with cherry or plum reductions on duck.

history.summary: Pfaffenberg is a Kremstal-classified Ried adjacent to the Wachau boundary. The Knoll family has worked parcels here for decades, producing what many consider their most complex Riesling. The classification is noted as Wachau DAC on the label but the site technically straddles the boundary.

milestones.event: First Pfaffenberg Riesling Smaragd bottled as a distinct cuvée from this boundary-straddling site

wines.description: Complex, age-worthy Smaragd Riesling from the Pfaffenberg site: yellow peach, graphite and chalky mineral with exceptional length and ageing potential.

wines.name: Emmerich Knoll Grüner Veltliner Federspiel

pairings.why: Light, refreshing acidity and pepper note pair seamlessly with delicate freshwater fish.
Fresh and herbal wine complements the cool, light salad.
Versatile food wine; acidity bridges marinated vegetables and charcuterie.

history.summary: The Federspiel tier was established by Vinea Wachau as the everyday drinking category (12-12.5% abv), accessible and meant for early consumption. Knoll's Federspiel Grüner demonstrates the estate's quality-first philosophy extends to every tier.

milestones.event: Federspiel tier formally defined by Vinea Wachau Nobilis Districtus; Knoll's entry-level Grüner becomes a flagship of the category

wines.description: Light, peppery Federspiel Grüner Veltliner from Emmerich Knoll. White pepper, green apple and herbal freshness in an everyday drinking style.

wines.name: Emmerich Knoll Riesling Federspiel

pairings.why: Light citrus acidity cuts through the prawn and sauce; the wine refreshes between bites.
Delicate citrus mineral notes are a classic partner for vegetable-forward Japanese cuisine.
Mineral freshness matches delicate seafood preparations without overpowering.

history.summary: The Federspiel Riesling is Knoll's most accessible and early-drinking wine: lighter in style, lower in alcohol and intended for consumption within a few years. It demonstrates that the estate applies rigour to all quality tiers.

milestones.event: Vinea Wachau Federspiel category codified; Knoll Riesling Federspiel becomes an estate-level entry cuvée

wines.description: Light, refreshing Federspiel Riesling from Emmerich Knoll. Lime blossom, citrus and mineral freshness for early drinking.

wines.name: Nikolaihof Vom Stein Riesling Smaragd
wines.oak_regime: Large old oak Fuder and barrels; no new oak; Demeter biodynamic cellar

pairings.why: Mineral austerity and firm acidity cut through the richness of caper butter and freshwater fish.
Earthy herb notes in the wine resonate with mushroom; acidity refreshes after the rich cheese.
The wine's tension and smokiness mirror the eel's intensity without being overwhelmed.

history.summary: The Saahs family converted Nikolaihof to biodynamic viticulture in 1971, making it one of the earliest Demeter-certified wine estates in Europe. The Vom Stein Riesling Smaragd comes from limestone-rich terraces near Mautern on the south bank of the Danube, yielding a structured, austere style that embodies the estate's commitment to natural farming.

milestones.event: Nikolaihof converts to Demeter biodynamic viticulture under Christine Saahs
Joins Vinea Wachau Nobilis Districtus; the Vom Stein Riesling becomes one of its founding Smaragd cuvées

wines.description: Demeter biodynamic Smaragd Riesling from the south-bank Vom Stein Ried: limestone mineral, lime blossom and herbal spice with firm, structured acidity.

wines.name: Nikolaihof Hefeabzug Grüner Veltliner Smaragd
wines.oak_regime: Large old oak Fuder; extended lees contact; no new oak

pairings.why: The wine's creamy, rich body and spice note can handle the intensity of organ meat.
Ripe pear and pepper notes echo roasted root vegetable sweetness.
Creamy, buttery wine texture matches hard aged cheese with nutty character.

history.summary: The Hefeabzug (direct-from-lees bottling) is a traditional Austrian practice that Nikolaihof has championed as part of their natural, intervention-minimal approach. The wine is bottled from the Fuder cask without fining or filtration, preserving the full creamy texture of the lees.

milestones.event: Hefeabzug Grüner Veltliner first released as a distinct cuvée showcasing the estate's sur-lies philosophy
The Hefeabzug wins recognition at Austrian national wine competitions for its distinctive textural style

wines.description: Sur-lies Smaragd Grüner Veltliner from Nikolaihof: creamy brioche texture, white pepper and ripe pear from extended Fuder ageing on lees without filtration.

wines.name: Nikolaihof Im Weingebirge Grüner Veltliner Smaragd
wines.oak_regime: Large old oak Fuder; natural yeasts; Demeter biodynamic

pairings.why: Fresh herb and citrus notes in the wine directly echo the cooking style.
The wine's herbal freshness complements light vegetable preparations.
White pepper and acidity cut through the rich, melted cheese.

history.summary: Im Weingebirge (in the wine mountains) is a name referencing the high-elevation Nikolaihof parcels above Mautern. The biodynamic estate emphasises the influence of lunar calendar and natural cellar practices in producing this cuvée.

milestones.event: Im Weingebirge introduced as the mid-tier Grüner Veltliner Smaragd cuvée from Nikolaihof's higher parcels

wines.description: Fresh, herb-driven Smaragd Grüner Veltliner from Nikolaihof's higher terraces: white pepper, green herbs and lemon curd with clean biodynamic expression.

wines.name: Nikolaihof Vom Stein Riesling Federspiel

pairings.why: Light, mineral Riesling matches the delicacy of freshwater fish without overpowering.
Fresh citrus and herbal notes complement the cool cucumber and dill pairing.
Lower alcohol and refreshing acidity make this ideal as an aperitivo.

history.summary: The Federspiel tier from Nikolaihof demonstrates the estate's philosophy of producing wines for everyday pleasure alongside their age-worthy Smaragd range. The biodynamic approach is applied equally at all quality levels.

milestones.event: First Vom Stein Riesling Federspiel produced as a lighter, lower-alcohol wine for early drinking

wines.description: Biodynamic Federspiel Riesling from Nikolaihof's Vom Stein site: light, refreshing lime and mineral character for early drinking.

wines.name: Nikolaihof Vinothek Riesling
wines.oak_regime: Large old oak Fuder; barrel-aged 5+ years before release

pairings.why: The wine's complexity and evolved texture call for a refined fish preparation.
Evolved honey and apricot notes in the wine echo this classic cheese-and-honey combination.
Rich, concentrated wine with mineral depth is a worthy partner to fatty foie gras.

history.summary: The Vinothek releases at Nikolaihof are inspired by traditional Austrian practice of holding wine in barrel for years before selling. The ancient Fuder cellar (dating to Roman times) provides the stable low-temperature environment for extended ageing. The wine is released only when the Saahs family judges it ready.

milestones.event: Vinothek Riesling programme launched: wines held in barrel minimum 5 years before release
International recognition of the Vinothek concept as a distinctive Austrian wine-ageing tradition

wines.description: Extended barrel-aged Riesling from Nikolaihof's ancient Fuder cellar, released only after five or more years. Petrol, dried apricot and evolved mineral complexity.

wines.name: Alzinger Riesling Smaragd Steinertal

pairings.why: Primary-rock mineral and racy acidity provide the perfect counterpoint to rich shellfish.
The wine's mineral tension and length are a classic pairing with Danube fish.
Racy acidity and mineral freshness are a natural partner to briny oysters.

history.summary: Leo Alzinger established his estate's reputation through rigorous single-vineyard viticulture, particularly the Steinertal Ried in Unterloiben. The estate name became synonymous with primary-rock mineral precision in Wachau Riesling. Daughter Katharina Alzinger-Kittel now continues the tradition with the same philosophy.

milestones.event: Leo Alzinger produces his first Steinertal Riesling Smaragd as a distinct single-Ried cuvée
Katharina Alzinger-Kittel takes over winemaking, maintaining the estate's minimalist, site-expressive approach

wines.description: Mineral Smaragd Riesling from Alzinger's Steinertal Ried: primary-rock stone, lime oil and herbal spice with racy acidity and long finish.

wines.name: Alzinger Grüner Veltliner Smaragd Loibenberg
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel and old Fuder; no new oak

pairings.why: Pepper spice and acidity cut through the breaded veal in this classic Wachau pairing.
Rich pork finds a counterbalance in the wine's firm acidity and spicy pepper.
Citrus and mineral notes echo the lemon-dressed fish preparation.

history.summary: Alzinger works parcels in the Loibenberg Ried that produce a richer, fuller style of Grüner Veltliner compared to the estate's linear Rieslings. This cuvée entered the range as a complement to the Steinertal Riesling, demonstrating the estate's dual expertise in both grape varieties.

milestones.event: First Loibenberg Grüner Veltliner Smaragd from Alzinger; estate builds reputation across both Grüner and Riesling

wines.description: Full-bodied Smaragd Grüner Veltliner from Alzinger's Loibenberg parcels: white pepper, citrus, stone fruit and firm mineral backbone.

wines.name: Alzinger Grüner Veltliner Smaragd Höhereck

pairings.why: The wine's mineral and herbal freshness echoes the wine-steamed mussels perfectly.
Herbal and pepper notes in the wine stand up to light Asian seasonings.
Herb notes and citrus acidity in the wine match the fresh tang of goat's cheese.

history.summary: Höhereck is a mid-altitude site in the Unterloiben area producing a crisper, more herbal style of Grüner Veltliner. It serves as the entry point to Alzinger's Grüner Veltliner range before the more concentrated Loibenberg Smaragd.

milestones.event: Höhereck Grüner Veltliner Smaragd introduced as a lighter-styled single-Ried cuvée

wines.description: Herb-driven Smaragd Grüner Veltliner from Alzinger's Höhereck site: white pepper, lemon balm and mineral freshness.

wines.name: Alzinger Grüner Veltliner Smaragd Steinertal

pairings.why: Pepper-spice and mineral notes cut through cured salmon richness.
Mineral freshness and herbal notes complement spring vegetable preparations.
Acidity cuts the richness; herbal wine notes echo the herb crust.

history.summary: The Steinertal site produces contrasting expressions from Riesling and Grüner Veltliner at Alzinger. The Grüner version is more angular and mineral than the Loibenberg, closer in spirit to the Riesling from the same Ried.

milestones.event: First Steinertal Grüner Veltliner Smaragd produced separately from the Riesling

wines.description: Angular, mineral Smaragd Grüner Veltliner from Alzinger's Steinertal: pepper spice, lime zest and primary-rock mineral character.

wines.name: Tegernseerhof Grüner Veltliner Smaragd Loibenberg Bergdistel
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel and large old oak Fuder; no new oak

pairings.why: The wine's weight and pepper spice are calibrated for rich, roasted white meats.
Classic Wachau pairing: the wine's spice and mineral lift bridge asparagus bitterness.
The Danube's freshwater fish finds an ideal partner in this mineral-rich Grüner.

history.summary: The Bergdistel (mountain thistle) label was created by Martin Mittelbach to distinguish his flagship Loibenberg Grüner Veltliner Smaragd. The name references the wild thistles that grow on the steep Loibenberg terraces.

milestones.event: Bergdistel label introduced for the estate's flagship Loibenberg Grüner Veltliner Smaragd
Martin Mittelbach receives DÖW (Distilled Wines of Austria) recognition; Bergdistel becomes the estate's calling card

wines.description: Estate signature Smaragd Grüner Veltliner from Tegernseerhof's Loibenberg parcels, labelled Bergdistel: ripe peach, white pepper and full-bodied mineral character.

wines.name: Tegernseerhof Riesling Smaragd Steinertal

pairings.why: Racy mineral Riesling cuts through rich cream while echoing the crustacean sweetness.
Lime-mineral intensity and racy acidity suit delicate raw fish preparations.
Primary-rock mineral and sharp acidity are a classic oyster pairing.

history.summary: The Steinertal Ried is shared among Loiben-area producers; Tegernseerhof's version emphasises the site's mineral austerity. Martin Mittelbach produces a tighter, more linear Riesling from these parcels compared to the estate's richer Grüner Veltliner.

milestones.event: First Tegernseerhof Steinertal Riesling Smaragd as a distinct single-Ried cuvée

wines.description: Taut, mineral Smaragd Riesling from Tegernseerhof's Steinertal parcels: crushed stone, lime blossom and racy acidity with exceptional length.

wines.name: Tegernseerhof Grüner Veltliner Smaragd Frauenweingärten

pairings.dish: Chicken breast with cream and mushroom
pairings.why: The wine's fullness and pepper spice support creamy poultry sauces.
Spiced pear and citrus complement baked freshwater fish.
Ripe fruit and medium acidity make a versatile match for a range of cheeses.

history.summary: Frauenweingärten (women's vineyards) is a site with historical associations with Benedictine religious communities in the Wachau. Tegernseerhof's parcels here produce a more approachable, early-drinking Smaragd style.

milestones.event: First Frauenweingärten Grüner Veltliner Smaragd as a standalone cuvée

wines.description: Richer, fruit-forward Smaragd Grüner Veltliner from the Frauenweingärten Ried: spiced pear, citrus blossom and full body.

wines.name: Tegernseerhof Grüner Veltliner Federspiel Dürnstein

pairings.why: Light acidity and crisp character cut through fried batter effortlessly.
A relaxed everyday food match for this easy-drinking Federspiel.
Herbal freshness and crisp acidity work seamlessly with dressed greens.

history.summary: The Dürnstein Federspiel is Tegernseerhof's approachable entry-level cuvée, labelled under the Dürnstein village appellation. It reflects the estate's conviction that quality viticulture can deliver excellent wines at every tier.

milestones.event: Dürnstein Federspiel introduced as the estate's lower-tier everyday-drinking Grüner Veltliner

wines.description: Light, peppery Federspiel Grüner Veltliner from Tegernseerhof: fresh herbs, crisp apple and white pepper for everyday enjoyment.

wines.name: Domäne Wachau Grüner Veltliner Smaragd Achleiten
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel and large old oak; no new oak

pairings.dish: Grilled veal chop with herb butter
pairings.why: Pepper spice and full body match the richness of veal with herb butter.
A Danube fish classic: the wine's mineral breadth complements freshwater fish.
Classic Wachau pairing: pepper and acidity balance the egg-butter sauce.

history.summary: The Achleiten Ried in Weissenkirchen is one of the Wachau's most-celebrated crus, with soil composed of gneiss and amphibolite that produces distinctively spicy, concentrated Grüner Veltliner. Domäne Wachau as a cooperative controls significant Achleiten parcels and has bottled this cuvée since the cooperative's reorganisation after World War II.

milestones.event: Winzergenossenschaft Wachau founded; Achleiten parcels among the first sites cultivated
Cooperative rebranded as Domäne Wachau; Achleiten Smaragd retains flagship status

wines.description: Domäne Wachau's flagship Grüner Veltliner Smaragd from the celebrated Achleiten Ried: white pepper, ripe peach and full mineral texture.

wines.name: Domäne Wachau Riesling Smaragd Kellerberg

pairings.why: Mineral acidity and stone fruit notes complement sweet crab in rich bisque.
Classic Austrian pairing: mineral Riesling with Danube carp, trout and perch.
The wine's citrus and mineral lift balance the smoked richness.

history.summary: The Kellerberg Ried above Loiben is shared among estates; Domäne Wachau's large cooperative holdings include premium parcels on this site. The cooperative's size allows consistent Kellerberg Riesling Smaragd production across vintages.

milestones.event: Cooperative begins bottling Kellerberg Riesling Smaragd as a standalone site wine
Under the Domäne Wachau name, Kellerberg becomes part of the Premium Ried selection

wines.description: Single-vineyard Kellerberg Riesling Smaragd from Domäne Wachau: slate mineral, apricot and lime on a mineral-driven palate.

wines.name: Domäne Wachau Riesling Smaragd Singerriedel

pairings.why: Granite mineral and racy acidity are a textbook oyster pairing.
Complex mineral tension and citrus freshness cut through the richness of thermidor sauce.
Racy mineral Riesling is a natural pairing for high-quality raw fish.

history.summary: Singerriedel is a historic Ried above Spitz on steep gneiss and granite soils. Domäne Wachau controls significant parcels here and has made this one of their prestige single-vineyard Riesling cuvées. The site's cooler exposure produces a distinctly tighter, more mineral style than the Kellerberg.

milestones.event: Singerriedel Riesling Smaragd first bottled separately by the cooperative
Elevated to Premium Ried tier under the Domäne Wachau brand

wines.description: Racy, granite-mineral Smaragd Riesling from Domäne Wachau's Singerriedel parcels near Spitz: citrus zest, crushed granite and exceptional tension.

wines.name: Domäne Wachau Grüner Veltliner Federspiel Terrassen

pairings.why: A classic Austrian match: the light, peppery Grüner pairs effortlessly with freshwater fish.
The wine's crisp acidity and light body complement the tangy Austrian potato salad.
Easy-drinking and versatile, this Federspiel handles light cold cuts without conflict.

history.summary: The Terrassen Federspiel (terrace Federspiel) is named for the UNESCO-listed stone-terrace vineyards that characterise the Wachau landscape. It is widely distributed among Grüner Veltliners and an accessible entry into the region's distinctive style.

milestones.event: Terrassen range launched as the cooperative's entry-level offering across multiple grape varieties
Rebranded under Domäne Wachau; Terrassen Federspiel Grüner becomes the global face of the estate

wines.description: Austria's widely distributed entry-level Grüner Veltliner: Domäne Wachau Terrassen Federspiel. Light-bodied, white-pepper and crisp apple at an accessible price.
